464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** – no documented path for complaints (class quality, coach behaviour, billing disputes, facility issues), who decides resolution level, refund/credit authority, retention follow-up
- **Churn root-cause analysis & win-back** – no process to capture why members leave, segment them, or trigger targeted re-engagement campaigns
- **Coach performance management & removal** – no SOP for handling underperformance, member feedback loops, probation, or exit
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no documented process for daily/weekly cash/card reconciliation, invoice verification, or fraud prevention
- **Health & safety incident reporting** – no formal log for injuries, near-misses, equipment failures, or regulatory notification
- **Member data privacy & GDPR compliance** – no documented process for data requests, retention schedules, or breach response
- **New class/programme launch** – no standardised rollout process (testing, coach prep, marketing timeline, success metrics)
- **Staffing recruitment & onboarding** – likely exists piecemeal; no formal hiring rubric, probation checkpoints, or induction timeline

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding SOP** – confirm it covers goal-setting, baseline metrics, payment setup, class booking rules, and initial coach pairing; likely missing retention check-ins at 4 weeks & 12 weeks
- **Coach daily/weekly SOPs** – verify they reflect current class format, member communication standards, and how coaches log attendance/feedback; may lack guidance on spotting at-risk members
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – fill the two gaps (Email Newsletter, Blog Post); also audit existing ones for lead nurturing, trial-to-paid conversion tracking, and referral incentives
- **Facilities SOPs (4/4)

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## 🎯 REVENUE — PRIORITY ACTION

**Single biggest issue: June revenue down 12.2% month-on-month (£20,199 → £17,711) despite 122 active clients & zero churn.** This is a red flag — the larger studio should drive *more* revenue, not less.

- **Pricing audit urgently needed.** AR/M (average revenue per member) collapsed from £165.56 (May) to £120.48 (June) — a £45 per-member drop. Check if June classes had discount promotions, trial rates, or inactive members still on books. Target: restore to £160+ minimum; at 147 members that's £23,520 monthly revenue (+£5,809). This is low-hanging fruit.
- **Upsell & class capacity gap.** You now have 2× studio space but Q2 (April–June: £56,465 total) only slightly outpaced Q1 (Jan–Mar: £47,149). Member growth 108→147 (+36%) hasn't translated proportionally to revenue growth. Implement tiered membership (standard/premium/personal training packages) and fill off-peak classes via targeted email to existing members (+15–20% capacity = +£2–3k monthly).
- **New revenue line: retail or partner services.** June expenses spiked non-people costs to £9,390 without corresponding revenue lift. Consider branded apparel, supplements, or partner physio/nutrition services (20–30% margin, minimal headcount cost).

---

## 💷 EXPENSES — IMMEDIATE COST CONTROL

**Non-people expenses are the culprit.** Q2 average: £8,345/month vs Q1 average: £6,169/month — a 35% increase that's eroding profit despite higher member count.

- **Non-people expenses out of control:** June hit £9,390 (53% of revenue). Drill into April–June detail immediately: utilities, rent, insurance, supplies, software. Q1 held these to 42–45% of revenue. Target Q2 back to 40% (£7,084 in June) = +£2,306 straight-line profit. Likely culprits: new studio lease higher than expected, or double-running both locations briefly post-move.
- **People costs stable & right-sized.** June payroll £3,686 (20.8% of revenue) is acceptable; Q2 average 17.6% is healthy. No action needed here — this is your controllable fixed

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Move Grace's Build: Lower (17 sessions) to JoJo** – JoJo already leads this format and has capacity; this is JoJo's core strength and keeps quality consistent.

• **Assign Grace's Build: Upper (9 sessions) to Eilis** – Eilis needs more technical class experience before Tuesdays; Upper Body is less complex than Lower and builds her confidence on a mixed-week schedule.

• **Distribute Grace's Elevate (6 sessions) between JoJo and Eilis** – splits the load; Eilis gets exposure to this format, JoJo carries the heavier class portfolio she's already managing well.

• **Hand Grace's Strongevity and No Sweat Intro (6 sessions total) to Eilis** – these are lower-intensity formats ideal for Eilis to lead and boost her session count without overstretch.

• **Stop treating InBody scans as part of PT/class load** – reassign all 29 scans away from JoJo and Eilis to a rota or admin task; JoJo then drops to ~113 real coaching sessions, freeing ~20 hours per month for Head Coach oversight and member experience.

• **Once Grace clears to zero, review JoJo's PT load (44 sessions)** – she's carrying the bulk; grow Eilis's PT to 15–18 sessions over next two months to build her income and reduce dependency on one coach.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time:**

1. Add a paid 30-min "Open Studio" slot on Monday and Thursday mornings (your quietest days) — members can book PT-style for strength work, conditioning drills or form coaching in an otherwise empty studio, positioned as flexible alternative to full classes.

2. Host a weekly 45-min "Mobility + Strength Pairing" workshop on Saturday mornings (lowest attendance day) teaching how to prep for the Build classes — position it as a retention tool for members intimidated by your most popular offerings.

**For the quietest classes (Engine, Capacity, Barbelle, Strongevity, Games Night):**

1. Move Engine to Thursday evening instead of its current slot — it's a low-barrier cardio class that suits your quieter day, and promote it as a "no-lifting required" option to widen appeal beyond your strength-focused members.

2. Rebrand Games Night as a "social strength mixer" with a 2-for-1 intro offer targeting members who attend Build classes solo — position it as community-building rather than standalone.

3. Trial Barbelle and Strongevity as back-to-back offerings (same day, consecutive slots) to create a themed "evening" for niche audiences and reduce studio downtime between them.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Execute Your Service Menu — Here's How Grace Does This TODAY

• **Map revenue by interaction type first.** Before writing anything, list every way members currently pay you: monthly membership, PT sessions, class packages, nutrition coaching, challenges, merchandise. Then interview 3-5 of your best-paying members asking "what made you buy that?" You'll find patterns in what actually sells vs. what you *think* should sell. Your menu reflects reality, not assumptions.

• **Create two separate, brutally honest docs.** Coach version = what they can actually upsell without it being slimy (PT add-ons, nutrition, prog tracking); what margin you make; what support they need. Member version = clean, benefit-focused pricing with ONE clear tier structure (avoid 7 confusing options). Test the member version with 5 randoms outside The Forge—if they don't instantly get the difference between tiers, rewrite it.

• **Use pricing anchoring immediately.** Your top tier should feel slightly uncomfortable to you. Members anchor to the highest price they see. If your most expensive offering is weak, they'll never justify mid-tier spending. Price your premium PT/coaching package 20-30% higher than you think it can sell, then test it with your most engaged 20 members first.

• **Lock it into a one-pager with visuals, not a document.** Coaches get a laminated card. Members get an Instagram post + one-sheet. If it takes more than 30 seconds to explain what someone buys, it's too complicated and you're leaving money on the table.

What's your current highest-revenue service right now?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**INSIGHTS**

Your cost-per-lead has ballooned 350% (£6 to £27) while lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 to 4) across four months—this is a clear sign of creative fatigue, audience saturation, or both. Your close rate is holding steady (60–75%), so the sales conversion funnel isn't broken; you're simply not getting prospects into it. July's low spend (£109) suggests you've already throttled budget defensively, but you're now underfeeding a channel that still converts at 75% and delivers profit.

The real problem: you're running the same ads to the same small audience repeatedly, and that audience is exhausted. April's £6/lead came from fresh creative and broad reach. By July, Meta's algorithm has shown your ads to everyone remotely interested in women's fitness in Belfast—multiple times—and the remaining 4 leads are bargain-hunted stragglers or repeat clickers.

**ACTIONS**

1. **Pause current creative and test 3 new ad variants immediately.** One should be testimonial/transformation video (before-after or member story); one should be outcome-focused (confidence, strength, community—not just "6-week trial"); one should be objection-crushing (fear of judgment, newbie anxiety, time constraints). If you haven't refreshed creative in 6+ weeks, this alone will cut cost-per-lead back to £12–15.

2. **Rebuild audience intent layers.** Stop relying on broad "women interested in fitness" targeting. Create three separate ad sets: (a) Detailed targeting: women 25–45 interested in Weight Loss + Health + Women's Fitness (narrow, but intent-rich); (b) Lookalike audience based on your best 10 trial customers (April's 69 leads likely contained them); (c) Retargeting website visitors from the last 90 days—these are warm and cheap to reactivate. Split budget 50% / 30% / 20%.

3. **Reset budget to £300–350 for August with the new creative.** You're sitting at profit on 75% close rate; you can afford £18–20 cost-per-lead and

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Action Plan

## REVENUE — URGENT PRIORITY

• **Churn spike in May (11.9%) killed growth.** You gained 8 net members Jan–Apr, then lost momentum: May added only 18 but lost 17 to churn; June recovered to 8 net adds but churn still 3.2% (acceptable, but down from 3.5% in Apr). **Action:** Audit May cohort—why did 17 members quit? Run exit surveys via email/SMS; interview any who cite class times, equipment, or instructor quality. Implement a 30-day "at-risk" check-in (email + free class) for new joiners who don't hit 4 sessions in their first month.

• **Revenue-per-member is eroding.** Jun shows £107/member—your lowest since Nov (£119). This is *not* just churn noise; it suggests members are downgrading plans or paying lower rates. **Action:** Audit your pricing tier mix in TeamUp—what % are on entry-level vs premium? Segment Jun joiners: are they signing up at a discount? If yes, stop promotional pricing; if tiers have drifted, raise entry-level by £5–10/month (to £25–30) and bundle a personal training intro session as sweetener, not discount.

• **Quiet class utilisation → lost ancillary revenue.** At 148 members (Jun), you have capacity for 180–200 before quality drops. You're not filling off-peak slots (likely 6am, 10am, 2pm weekday slots). **Action:** Launch *lunchtime corporate partner classes* (offer local offices 5-class bulk discount) and *weekend kid's gym club* (parent + child sessions, £15/person on top of membership). Test 4 weeks; target £1,500 add-on revenue/month if uptake is 50%.

---

## EXPENSES — MATERIAL DRAG

• **Cash out in Mar (£23,328) and Apr (£39,015) suggests fixed + variable costs are misaligned to revenue.** Strip out Apr relocation spend; Mar shows £23.3k out vs £14.2k revenue = 164% cost ratio. **Action:** Pull P&L detail: rent/rates, utilities, payroll, insurance, equipment lease. Compare Mar headcount & hours to Jun. If you've kept Apr payroll levels (post-relocation) but Jun revenue hasn't grown proportionally (£15.7k), you're over-staffed. Model: target 45% cost-of-goods-sold +
